sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 31, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'moves player 4x4 avatar right by 6 cols to next chamber (VERIFIED steps 1,3,4)', 'DOWN': 'only shrinks HUD timer at [8,20] (step 2); untested at other positions', 'LEFT': 'untested', 'UP': 'untested', 'SPACE': 'untested'}
- Object taxonomy: {'blue_square_black_center': 'player avatar, 4x4 blue square with 1 black center pixel, 24 pixels total', 'blue_square_blue_center': 'target/goal, 6x6 blue square with 1 blue center pixel, 19 pixels, at bottom-right ~[49,43]-[55,49]', 'white_paths': 'maze corridors forming S-shaped path through black walls', 'red_path': 'red colored path on right side of maze, 82 pixels', 'white_square': 'white 5x5 square at [14,20]-[18,24], possibly another target or obstacle'}
- HUD map: {'row_63': 'blue timer strip, shrinks by 1 pixel per action (64→63→...)'}
- Win pattern: Reach the blue target square at bottom-right with the player avatar
Current level:
- World model: Maze with chambers connected by white corridors. Player is 4x4 blue square that jumps between chambers. Chambers in top row at cols 14, 20, 26, 32 (spaced 6 apart). White path forms S-shape: top-left → top-right → down right side → left → down → right to bottom. Player currently at [8,32] after 3 RIGHTs.
- Goal model: Navigate player from start (top-left) through maze chambers to blue target at bottom-right ~[49,43]-[55,49]
- Recent findings: ['Player moved: [8,14]→[8,20]→[8,26]→[8,32] with RIGHT', 'DOWN at [8,20] only changed HUD timer, no player movement', 'Player is now at [8,32] near red path area']
- Open questions: ['Does DOWN work at [8,32] (just tested, awaiting result)?', 'What is the full chamber connectivity graph?', 'Can LEFT reverse movement?', 'What does SPACE do?']
- Plan: Test DOWN at [8,32]. If it works, continue following the S-shaped white corridor path: down right side, then left, then down, then right to target. If not, try RIGHT more or LEFT back to find working DOWN position.
- Failed probes: ['DOWN at [8,20] - no player movement, only HUD timer changed (step 2)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 30 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
LEFT at [14,26] moved to [20,26] (down 6 rows), not left! This is very interesting. So from [14,26]:
- RIGHT → [20,26] (down 6 rows)
- LEFT → [20,26] (down 6 rows)
- UP → [8,26] (up 6 rows)
- DOWN → untested
So both RIGHT and LEFT from [14,26] go to [20,26]. This suggests that the movement is not simply left/right/up/down, but rather follows the maze path. From [14,26], the path goes down to [20,26], and both RIGHT and LEFT trigger this movement.
Now I'm at [20,26]. From here I know:
- LEFT → [20,20]
- RIGHT → untested (but earlier it didn't work)
- UP → [14,26]
- DOWN → failed earlier
Let me try DOWN from [20,26] again. Wait, I already tried it and it failed. Let me try RIGHT from [20,26] to see if it works now.
Actually, let me think about the maze structure more carefully. The white path forms an S-shape:
1. Top row: left to right
2. Down the right side
3. Left
4. Down
5. Right to bottom
The player seems to follow this path. From [14,26], the path goes down to [20,26]. From [20,26], the path goes left to [20,20]. From [20,20], the path might go down or right.
Let me try DOWN from [20,20] to see if it works. But I already tried it twice and it failed. Let me try LEFT from [20,20] to see if there's a position further left.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# LEFT at [14,26] moved to [20,26] (down). Player at [20,26]. Try LEFT to [20,20], then LEFT again.
def find_player(seg):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24 and n['children']:
            return n
    return None

p = find_player(current_frame.segmentation)
print(f"Player: {p['boundary'][0] if p else '?'}")

# Try LEFT to [20,20], then LEFT again
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "moves player right by 6 cols in top row, down by 6 rows from [14,26]", "DOWN": "works at [8,38] to move to [14,26]", "LEFT": "moves player left by 6 cols in lower maze, down by 6 rows from [14,26]", "UP": "moves player up 6 rows", "SPACE": "resets player to start [8,14]"}, "object_taxonomy": {"blue_square_black_center": "player avatar (4x4)", "blue_square_blue_center": "target/goal"}, "hud_map": {"row_63": "blue timer strip"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Player at [20,26]. LEFT at [14,26] moved to [20,26] (down). Both RIGHT and LEFT from [14,26] go down.", "goal_model": "Navigate to blue target at bottom right", "recent_findings": ["LEFT at [14,26] moved to [20,26] (down 6 rows)"], "open_questions": ["Does LEFT work at [20,20] to go further left?"], "current_plan": "Try LEFT from [20,20] to explore further", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["DOWN at [20,26]", "DOWN at [20,20] (twice)", "UP at [20,20]", "DOWN at [8,26]"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
